examples.concept: pure flow
examples.aiRule: Use pure flow for deterministic logic with no effects.

examples.concept: guarded flow
examples.aiRule: Use guarded flow when the flow performs declared effects.

examples.concept: secure flow
examples.aiRule: Use secure flow at trust boundaries such as HTTP/API requests.

examples.concept: fn helper
examples.aiRule: fn is a local helper defined inside a flow. It cannot declare effects.

examples.concept: let binding
examples.aiRule: let declares an immutable binding.

examples.concept: mut binding
examples.aiRule: mut declares a mutable binding that can be reassigned.

examples.concept: readonly parameter
examples.aiRule: readonly parameters cannot be reassigned inside the flow.

examples.concept: readonly local binding
unsafe let — boundary input
examples.aiRule: Data from external boundaries starts as unsafe.

examples.concept: Result return type
examples.aiRule: Use Result<T, E> for fallible operations instead of exceptions.

examples.concept: Option return type
examples.aiRule: Use Option<T> instead of null.

examples.concept: match on Result
examples.aiRule: Every match on Result must handle both Ok and Err.

examples.concept: match on Option
examples.aiRule: Every match on Option must handle both Some and None.

examples.concept: enum declaration
record type
type alias
branded domain type
examples.aiRule: Use Brand<String, Name> to make domain types distinct at compile time.

examples.concept: protected type qualifier
examples.aiRule: protected marks a validated but sensitive value.

examples.concept: redacted type qualifier
examples.aiRule: redacted marks a value that has been masked for safe output.

examples.concept: fn cannot be declared at top level
flow contract block with types, intent, and events
examples.aiRule: Use contract { } to group flow-local metadata — types, intent, and events — in one governed location.

examples.concept: top-level let bindings are rejected
examples.aiRule: All executable bindings belong inside flows. Top-level scope is for declarations only.

examples.concept: readable operator aliases — and, or, unless, is
examples.aiRule: Readable forms are aliases. 'and' = &&, 'or' = ||, 'unless' = if !cond, 'is' = ==. Same AST.

examples.concept: Int type
examples.aiRule: Int is the standard integer type in Galerina.

examples.concept: Decimal type
examples.aiRule: Use Decimal("...") constructor for exact decimal values. Avoids floating-point rounding.

examples.concept: String type
examples.aiRule: String holds UTF-8 text. Untrusted strings should be wrapped in unsafe let.

examples.concept: Bool type
examples.aiRule: Bool values are true or false.

examples.concept: Byte type
examples.aiRule: Byte holds a single 8-bit unsigned integer, often written as a hex literal.

examples.concept: Char type
examples.aiRule: Char holds a single Unicode character, written with single quotes.

examples.concept: protected Email domain type
examples.aiRule: Email values come from validate.email().

examples.concept: protected PatientId domain type
examples.aiRule: PatientId values come from validate.patientId().

examples.concept: protected NhsNumber domain type
examples.aiRule: NhsNumber values come from validate.nhsNumber().

examples.concept: direct assignment to protected Email is forbidden
examples.aiRule: You cannot assign an unvalidated String to a protected type. Use validate.email(...).

examples.concept: redacted Email label
examples.aiRule: Use redact() to convert a protected value to a redacted value for audit logging.

examples.concept: protected Email cannot be assigned to redacted Email directly
examples.aiRule: Use redact(email) to explicitly redact a protected value before assigning to a redacted binding.

examples.concept: Option<T> with Some value
examples.aiRule: Option<T> represents a value that may be absent. Some(value) wraps a present value.

examples.concept: Option<T> with None value
examples.aiRule: None represents the absence of a value in Option<T>.

examples.concept: Option<T> requires exactly one type parameter
examples.aiRule: Option takes exactly 1 type parameter.

examples.concept: Result<T,E> Ok variant
examples.aiRule: Result<T, E> represents success (Ok) or failure (Err). Use Ok(value) for success.

examples.concept: Result<T,E> Err variant
examples.aiRule: Use Err(errorValue) to represent a typed failure in Result<T,E>.

examples.concept: Result<T,E> requires exactly two type parameters
examples.aiRule: Result takes exactly 2 type parameters: the success type and the error type.

examples.concept: Auto type inference
examples.aiRule: Auto means infer the type from the initializer expression.

examples.concept: Auto requires an initializer
examples.aiRule: Auto cannot infer a type without an initializer. Provide an initial value.

examples.concept: Money<GBP> type
examples.aiRule: Use Money.gbp("...") to create GBP monetary values with exact precision.

examples.concept: Money<GBP> addition with same currency
examples.aiRule: Money<GBP> + Money<GBP> is valid and yields Money<GBP>.

examples.concept: cross-currency Money addition is forbidden
examples.aiRule: Money types with different currency parameters cannot be added together.

examples.concept: Money<GBP> multiplied by Decimal
examples.aiRule: Money<GBP> * Decimal is valid and scales the monetary amount.

examples.concept: Money<GBP> divided by Decimal
examples.aiRule: Money<GBP> / Decimal is valid and yields Money<GBP>.

examples.concept: Money multiplied by Money is forbidden
examples.aiRule: Money cannot be multiplied by Money. Use Decimal for scaling factors.

examples.concept: Money<GBP> / Money<GBP> yields Decimal
examples.aiRule: Dividing Money<GBP> by Money<GBP> produces a dimensionless Decimal ratio.

examples.concept: cross-currency Money ratio is forbidden
examples.aiRule: Money ratio requires matching currency parameters.

examples.concept: Tensor<Dtype, Shape> with static shape
examples.aiRule: Tensor requires two type parameters: element type and shape.

examples.concept: Tensor<Dtype, DynamicShape>
examples.aiRule: Use DynamicShape when tensor dimensions are not known at compile time.

examples.concept: Tensor requires exactly two type parameters
examples.aiRule: Tensor<Dtype, Shape> requires both element type and shape parameters.

examples.concept: ReadOnlyView<T> generic type
examples.aiRule: ReadOnlyView<T> exposes a collection without mutation methods.

examples.concept: ReadOnlyView requires exactly one type parameter
examples.aiRule: ReadOnlyView takes exactly 1 type parameter.

examples.concept: unknown type name produces FUNGI-TYPE-001
examples.aiRule: All types must be declared or imported. Unknown type names are compile errors.

examples.concept: type mismatch produces FUNGI-TYPE-002
examples.aiRule: The right-hand side of a binding must match the declared type.

examples.concept: protected value cannot be directly assigned to redacted binding
examples.aiRule: Use redact(email) to convert a protected value to a redacted value.

examples.concept: full protected -> redacted -> audit pattern
examples.aiRule: Redact protected values before writing to audit sinks.

examples.concept: Array.range static constructor
examples.aiRule: Use Array.range(from, to) to generate a sequence of integers.

examples.concept: Duration type and arithmetic
examples.aiRule: Duration is a time span — use Duration.ofMs, ofSeconds, ofMinutes, ofHours.

examples.concept: Result.sequence converts Array<Result<T,E>> to Result<Array<T>, E>
examples.aiRule: Use Result.sequence to collect all Ok values or short-circuit on the first Err.

examples.concept: trust and sensitivity are independent axes
examples.aiRule: unsafe let = trust state. protected = sensitivity state. These are orthogonal.

examples.concept: Bool, true/false, if/else, and/or/not, comparisons
examples.aiRule: Bool holds true or false. Use && and || for and/or. Use ! for not.

examples.concept: Duration, Timestamp, Duration.ofSeconds, add/subtract
examples.aiRule: Duration is a time span. Timestamp is a point in time.

examples.concept: pure flow has no effects
examples.aiRule: Pure flows cannot perform I/O or call effectful operations.

examples.concept: guarded flow declaring database.write effect
examples.aiRule: Declare database.write when writing to a database.

examples.concept: guarded flow declaring network.outbound effect
examples.aiRule: Declare network.outbound when making outbound HTTP or network calls.

examples.concept: flow declaring multiple effects
examples.aiRule: List all required effects in the contract effects block.

examples.concept: undeclared database.write effect
examples.aiRule: Every effect used in a flow body must be declared in the contract effects block.

examples.concept: undeclared network.outbound effect
examples.aiRule: http.get and similar calls require network.outbound to be declared.

examples.concept: pure flow cannot call an effectful flow
examples.aiRule: A pure flow cannot call any flow that declares effects.

examples.concept: guarded flow can call effectful flow when it declares the same effects
examples.aiRule: A guarded flow may call effectful flows provided it declares all required effects.

examples.concept: local fn as a pure helper inside a flow
examples.aiRule: fn is a local helper inside a flow. It cannot be declared at the top level.

examples.concept: local fn may use effects declared by the containing flow
examples.aiRule: Local fn may use effects only when the containing flow declares them.

examples.concept: local fn cannot use effects not declared by the containing flow
examples.aiRule: A local fn is bound by the containing flow's effect declarations.

examples.concept: local fn cannot declare its own effects
examples.aiRule: Only flow declarations may declare effects. Local fn cannot have with effects [...].

examples.concept: secure flow with full effects pattern
examples.aiRule: Secure flows require audit.write alongside operational effects.

examples.concept: secure flow calls AuditLog.write but does not declare audit.write
examples.aiRule: AuditLog.write requires audit.write to be declared in the effect list.

examples.concept: effect propagation through a flow call chain
examples.aiRule: A flow calling another flow must declare all effects required by the callee.

examples.concept: caller missing effect required by callee
examples.aiRule: If a callee declares an effect, the caller must declare it too.

examples.concept: filesystem.read in a pure flow is forbidden
examples.aiRule: Pure flows cannot perform any I/O, including filesystem reads.

examples.concept: guarded flow with filesystem.read effect
examples.aiRule: Declare filesystem.read when reading from the filesystem.

examples.concept: non-canonical effect name is rejected
examples.aiRule: Effect names must use the canonical dotted form (e.g. network.outbound, not network).

examples.concept: comprehensive effects example showing all patterns
examples.aiRule: Declare every effect used. pure = no effects. guarded/secure = explicit effect list in contract.

examples.concept: HTTP request data enters as unsafe
examples.aiRule: Request data starts as unsafe. Always use unsafe let for values from request.body.

examples.concept: file content enters as unsafe
examples.aiRule: External files are unsafe. Use unsafe let for data read from the filesystem.

examples.concept: environment variables enter as unsafe
examples.aiRule: Environment values are unsafe. Treat them as untrusted external input.

examples.concept: validating unsafe email to protected Email
examples.aiRule: Email values come from validate.email(). The ? propagates validation failure.

examples.concept: validating unsafe patientId to protected PatientId
examples.aiRule: PatientId values come from validate.patientId().

examples.concept: validating unsafe nhsNumber to protected NhsNumber
examples.aiRule: NhsNumber values come from validate.nhsNumber().

examples.concept: assigning String directly to protected Email is forbidden
examples.aiRule: Cannot assign String directly to Email. Use validate.email(...).

examples.concept: redact() converts protected Email to redacted Email
examples.aiRule: Protected values become redacted through redact(). Use for audit logging.

examples.concept: redact() converts protected PatientId to redacted PatientId
examples.aiRule: Use redact() to safely prepare protected PatientId for audit logs.

examples.concept: protected value cannot be directly assigned to redacted binding
examples.aiRule: Protected values must be explicitly redacted. Use redact(email).

examples.concept: safe audit log write with redacted value
examples.aiRule: Protected -> Redacted -> Audit is the canonical Galerina trust flow for sensitive data.

examples.concept: unsafe value reaches audit sink without validation
examples.aiRule: Unsafe boundary data must be validated before reaching any governed sink including AuditLog.

examples.concept: passing unsafe value to audit sink
examples.aiRule: Unsafe values must be validated before reaching any sink.

examples.concept: protected value is safe to write to database
examples.aiRule: Validated protected values can be stored in a database sink with database.write declared.

examples.concept: unsafe value cannot be written to a database sink
examples.aiRule: Unsafe values cannot be passed to database sinks. Validate first.

examples.concept: protected value may be sent over network (policy dependent)
examples.aiRule: Protected values are not automatically forbidden from network sends. Governance policy decides.

examples.concept: unsafe value crossing a trust boundary via network
examples.aiRule: Unsafe values cannot cross trust boundaries. Validate before sending externally.

examples.concept: redacted value is safe to send to audit endpoints
examples.aiRule: Redacted values are safe to transmit to audit or logging endpoints.

examples.concept: direct equality comparison on secrets is forbidden
examples.aiRule: Never compare secrets with ==. Use constantTimeEquals(...) to prevent timing attacks.

examples.concept: constantTimeEquals for safe secret comparison
examples.aiRule: Use constantTimeEquals(a, b) for all secret value comparisons.

examples.concept: secret value sent to console sink is forbidden
examples.aiRule: SecureString values cannot be printed to the console. Redact first or omit.

examples.concept: secret value sent to console sink is forbidden
examples.aiRule: Never log secret values (API keys, passwords, tokens).

examples.concept: complete canonical trust flow chain
examples.aiRule: This is the canonical Galerina trust flow: unsafe -> validate -> protected -> redact -> audit.

examples.concept: multiple protected values all validated and redacted in audit
examples.aiRule: Each protected value must be individually validated and individually redacted for audit.

examples.concept: comprehensive security example showing all patterns
examples.aiRule: Full security pattern: trust boundaries, validation gates, protected types, redaction, safe sinks.

examples.concept: string taint propagation (SQL injection pattern)
examples.aiRule: String concatenation with unsafe input produces a tainted string — do not send to governed sinks.

examples.concept: SecureString serialization denied
examples.aiRule: SecureString must not be passed to json.encode or serialize functions.

examples.concept: intent declaration on a guarded flow
examples.aiRule: Use intent to describe what the flow is allowed to do.

examples.concept: secure flow with full intent declaration
examples.aiRule: Intent on a secure flow is the primary governance signal for patient data operations.

examples.concept: intent mismatch when flow behaviour contradicts declared intent
examples.aiRule: Flow behaviour must be consistent with declared intent.

examples.concept: compute target with remote.execution denied
examples.aiRule: Use deny [remote.execution] to prevent a flow from being dispatched to a remote compute target.

examples.concept: compute target remote violates governance policy
examples.aiRule: Specifying remote as the compute target when governance denies it is a policy violation.

examples.concept: authority block for sharing protected data externally
examples.aiRule: Use an authority block to declare permission to share protected data externally.

examples.concept: sending protected data externally without authority block
examples.aiRule: Protected data cannot be sent externally without an authority block granting permission.

examples.concept: delete flow with full audit evidence
examples.aiRule: Destructive operations on sensitive data require audit evidence. Declare audit.write and call AuditLog.write.

examples.concept: intent requires audit but no audit.write sink used
examples.aiRule: If intent or policy requires audit evidence, AuditLog.write must be called.

examples.concept: full governed execution plan for a ML inference flow
examples.aiRule: Combine intent, compute target, and audit for fully governed ML inference flows.

examples.concept: policy block declaring allowed purpose for data sharing
examples.aiRule: Use a policy block to declare allowed data-sharing purposes for patient communications.

examples.concept: declared purpose does not match observed template use
examples.aiRule: The template used in a communication flow must match the declared purpose.

examples.concept: comprehensive governance example showing all patterns
examples.aiRule: Governance = intent + effects + compute target + authority + policy + audit evidence.

examples.concept: flow contract on secure flow with governance intent
examples.aiRule: secure flows should use contract.intent to declare their governance purpose explicitly.

examples.concept: reusable contract set applied to a flow
examples.aiRule: Use contract set to define reusable governance templates.

examples.concept: global event declaration + flow emission + FUNGI-EVENT-001
examples.aiRule: Declare events globally with 'event X' before emitting with 'emit X'.

examples.concept: contract set use resolution — FUNGI-GOV-011 and FUNGI-GOV-012
examples.aiRule: 'use SetName' inside a flow contract must resolve to a declared 'contract set SetName'.

examples.concept: contract errors block — returns, map, expose, redact, audit
examples.aiRule: The errors {} block declares which errors a flow can return and how to map or redact them.

examples.concept: contract response.denies — FUNGI-GOV-003
examples.aiRule: When contract.response.denies lists a field, the flow body must not include it in the response.

examples.concept: contract context block — require actor, require trace_id, correct usage
examples.aiRule: When contract.context declares require fields, the flow body must access them.

examples.concept: contract timeouts and retries blocks
examples.aiRule: The timeouts block sets a hard deadline and per-effect timeouts.

examples.concept: contract limits and privacy blocks
examples.aiRule: The limits block caps resource consumption (request size, batch size, memory).

examples.concept: contract observability block — trace, measure, count, deny patterns
examples.aiRule: The observability block declares what to trace, measure, count, and deny in logs.

examples.concept: gold standard contract — named result type, full contract, raw->protected->redact->audit
examples.aiRule: This is the canonical gold standard pattern.

examples.concept: FUNGI-CONTEXT-001 — context require actor declared but never accessed
examples.aiRule: When contract.context declares require actor but the flow body never reads context.actor, FUNGI-CONTEXT-001 is raised.

examples.concept: all 16 contract sections in canonical order — minimal but complete template
examples.aiRule: Canonical contract section order: types -> intent -> request -> response -> context -> model -> effects -> timeouts -> retries -> limits -> privacy -> errors -> rules -> observability -> events -> audit

examples.concept: compute target selection with ordered preference and fallback
examples.aiRule: Use prefer [...] to express hardware preference; always declare fallback cpu as the final safety net.

examples.concept: denying remote execution in a secure credit-scoring flow
examples.aiRule: Use deny [remote.execution] for data that must not leave the device.

examples.concept: explicit GPU targeting with CPU fallback
examples.aiRule: Explicitly target gpu when the workload is known to saturate the GPU; always add fallback cpu for portability.

examples.concept: tensor shape encoded in the type signature
examples.aiRule: Encode tensor rank and dimensions as static type parameters so shape mismatches are caught at compile time.

examples.concept: dynamic shape tensor for variable-length inference
examples.aiRule: Use DynamicShape when batch size or sequence length varies at runtime.

examples.concept: Tensor requires exactly two type parameters: element type and shape
examples.aiRule: Tensor<T, Shape> requires both an element type and a shape argument; omitting either is a type error.

examples.concept: fixed-length vector type for embeddings
examples.aiRule: Use Vector<T, N> for fixed-length one-dimensional arrays, such as embedding outputs.

examples.concept: matrix type for weight tensors
examples.aiRule: Use Matrix<T, Rows, Cols> for two-dimensional numeric tables such as weight matrices.

examples.concept: photonic compute target with classical fallback chain
examples.aiRule: Include photonic first in the preference list when optical hardware is available; always provide a classical fallback chain.

examples.concept: mandatory fallback declaration when targeting npu or gpu
examples.aiRule: Always declare fallback cpu when targeting npu or gpu.

examples.concept: multiplying Money by a Decimal rate
examples.aiRule: Multiply Money<C> by Decimal to scale an amount; the result preserves the currency type.

examples.concept: adding Money values of different currencies is a type error
examples.aiRule: Never add or subtract Money<C1> and Money<C2> directly; convert to a common currency first.

examples.concept: dividing Money by a Decimal value for precise splitting
examples.aiRule: Use Decimal arithmetic for all monetary division to avoid floating-point rounding errors.

examples.concept: full AI text classification flow with NPU targeting and governance
examples.aiRule: Combine compute target best with deny [remote.execution] to enforce on-device AI inference.

examples.concept: complete governed AI inference flow with audit, hardware targeting, and deny clause
examples.aiRule: The summary pattern combines secure flow, protected types, compute target, deny, audit, and Result propagation.

examples.concept: Tensor arithmetic requires shape compatibility
Cross-currency arithmetic is denied
ai.inference without compute target preference
examples.aiRule: Flows using ai.inference should declare a compute target preference for NPU or GPU.

examples.concept: Money<C> * Decimal is valid scaling
examples.aiRule: Money<C> * Decimal produces Money<C>. Decimal is the correct type for rates/percentages.

examples.concept: pure statistical computation using Array.reduce and map
examples.aiRule: Use Array.reduce to fold a collection into a single value.

examples.concept: basic AI embedding flow returning a typed tensor
examples.aiRule: Every flow that calls an embedding model must declare the ai.inference effect.

examples.concept: calling an AI model without declaring the required ai.inference effect
examples.aiRule: Declare effects [ai.inference] on every flow that calls a machine learning model.

examples.concept: protected PII validated and passed through an AI risk model with redacted audit
examples.aiRule: Always validate raw input into a protected type before passing it to an AI model.

examples.concept: batch AI inference over an array of inputs
examples.aiRule: Use Array.map with ? propagation to run batch inference and surface the first error.

examples.concept: AI inference with remote execution denied for patient data governance
examples.aiRule: Pair ai.inference with deny [remote.execution] for any flow that handles patient data.

examples.concept: secure AI flow that declares audit.write but omits the AuditLog.write call
examples.aiRule: If a secure flow declares audit.write, it must call AuditLog.write before returning.

examples.concept: shape-typed model input and output tensors using a batch dimension
examples.aiRule: Use a named Batch dimension to express that tensor rank is fixed but batch size is variable.

examples.concept: passing a SecureString to an AI model is a secret-exposure error
examples.aiRule: Never pass a SecureString or secret-typed value to an AI model call.

examples.concept: embedding flow with NPU/GPU preference and CPU fallback
examples.aiRule: Declare compute fallback cpu on all embedding flows to allow CPU-only deployment.

examples.concept: protected patient data redacted in AI audit log entry
examples.aiRule: Always wrap protected values in redact() before passing them to AuditLog.write.

examples.concept: AnyTensor for dynamically loaded models with unknown shape
examples.aiRule: Use AnyTensor only when the tensor shape cannot be known at compile time.

examples.concept: Tensor type with missing shape argument raises FUNGI-TYPE-009
examples.aiRule: Tensor requires exactly two type parameters; omitting the shape is always a compile error.

examples.concept: local-only AI classification denying both remote execution and network outbound
examples.aiRule: Use deny [remote.execution, network.outbound] for air-gapped or strict data-residency deployments.

examples.concept: a pure flow calling an ai.inference flow inherits that effect and must declare it
examples.aiRule: Effects propagate upward; a caller that does not declare an effect used by its callee receives FUNGI-EFFECT-002.

examples.concept: complete AI governance pattern — healthcare risk scoring with PII, audit, and compute governance
examples.aiRule: The complete AI pattern is: secure flow + protected input + compute target + deny + ai.inference + audit.write + redact.

examples.concept: Unsafe input cannot flow directly to AI model
ai.inference effect must be declared
flow contract with AI intent for IGO optimisation
examples.aiRule: AI flows should use contract.intent to enable IGO.

examples.concept: flow contract with audit requirement for signed attestation
examples.aiRule: 'require signed attestation' in audit {} block triggers Ed25519 signing of the build artifact.

examples.concept: full AI classify flow — ClassifierModel, compute target, protected input, redacted audit
examples.aiRule: AI classification flows should declare contract.model { uses ClassifierModel }, compute target prefer [npu, gpu, cpu], and always redact protected inputs in audit log entries.

examples.concept: explicit CPU compute target
examples.aiRule: Use compute target cpu for workloads that are latency-sensitive on small inputs or that require deterministic single-threaded execution.

examples.concept: explicit GPU compute target with CPU fallback
examples.aiRule: Use compute target gpu for large dense tensor workloads; always add fallback cpu.

examples.concept: explicit NPU compute target with GPU fallback
examples.aiRule: Use compute target npu for on-device edge inference; fall back to gpu before cpu for performance.

examples.concept: WebAssembly compute target for browser or sandboxed execution
examples.aiRule: Use compute target wasm when the flow must run inside a browser or a WASM sandbox.

examples.concept: photonic (optical) compute target with GPU fallback
examples.aiRule: Use compute target photonic for extremely high-throughput linear algebra; always include a classical fallback.

examples.concept: quantum compute target with classical CPU fallback
examples.aiRule: Use compute target quantum for quantum-native algorithms; fallback cpu runs a classical simulation.

examples.concept: compute target best with ordered preference list and fallback
examples.aiRule: best selects the best available target at runtime from the preference list in order.

examples.concept: denying remote.execution placement category
examples.aiRule: deny [remote.execution] blocks any remote dispatch, regardless of what appears in the prefer list.

examples.concept: multiple denied target categories in a single deny clause
examples.aiRule: List all denied categories in a single deny [...] clause to make the exclusions explicit and auditable.

examples.concept: adaptive runtime block that learns from workload to optimise batching and warmup
examples.aiRule: runtime adaptive enables workload-learning optimisations within governance constraints.

examples.concept: deterministic runtime for regulated environments requiring identical execution planning
examples.aiRule: Use runtime deterministic for compliance environments requiring identical execution planning.

examples.concept: photonic preference with full classical fallback chain
examples.aiRule: When photonic is preferred, provide a full fallback chain npu > gpu > cpu to maximise portability.

examples.concept: quantum preference with GPU simulation and CPU fallback
examples.aiRule: List gpu before cpu in the quantum fallback chain to enable GPU-accelerated quantum simulation.

examples.concept: omitting the fallback clause from a non-cpu compute target is an error
examples.aiRule: Every compute target other than cpu requires an explicit fallback clause.

examples.concept: complete compute target governance — prefer, deny, fallback, adaptive runtime, and audit
examples.aiRule: The full target pattern combines prefer, deny, fallback, runtime mode, and governance effects.

examples.concept: compute target without fallback
healthcare PII governance pattern — create patient record with NHS number, protected types, and audit
examples.aiRule: All healthcare flows must validate raw PII into protected types, declare pii.write and audit.write effects, and redact in audit.

examples.concept: passing a protected PatientId directly to a log call without redaction
examples.aiRule: Never pass a protected value to a log or audit call without first applying redact().

examples.concept: payment governance flow with approved merchant, Money type, and audit
examples.aiRule: All payment flows must declare payment.charge and audit.write effects and include an intent clause.

examples.concept: adding Money<GBP> and Money<USD> without currency conversion is a type error
examples.aiRule: Cross-currency addition is always a compile error; use an explicit FX conversion before adding.

examples.concept: financial tax calculation using Money and Decimal
examples.aiRule: Use Decimal for all tax rate multiplication to avoid floating-point rounding in financial flows.

examples.concept: accessing PII without declaring the pii.read effect
examples.aiRule: Declare effects [pii.read] on every flow that reads personally identifiable information.

examples.concept: handling HIPAA PHI without declaring the phi.read effect
examples.aiRule: Declare effects [phi.read] on every flow that accesses HIPAA Protected Health Information.

examples.concept: secure flow that declares audit.write but never calls AuditLog.write
examples.aiRule: AuditLog.write must be called on every return path in a flow that declares audit.write.

examples.concept: multiple PII types in one flow — all validated as protected and all redacted in audit
examples.aiRule: When a flow handles multiple PII types, validate each into its own protected type and redact each separately in the audit entry.

examples.concept: authority block authorises sharing protected PatientData with an external service
examples.aiRule: Use an authority block to explicitly grant permission for protected data to cross a trust boundary.

examples.concept: sharing protected data across a trust boundary without an authority block
examples.aiRule: Sharing a protected value with an external service without an authority block is a governance violation.

examples.concept: policy block declares the permitted purpose for data use in a communication flow
examples.aiRule: Use a policy block with a purpose declaration to document and enforce the lawful basis for processing patient contact data.

examples.concept: declared purpose does not match the template used in the flow body
examples.aiRule: The template or action used in the flow body must be consistent with the purpose declared in the policy block.

examples.concept: flow requiring a module capability that has not been accepted in the supply-chain manifest
examples.aiRule: All external module capabilities must be listed and accepted in the supply-chain manifest before use.

examples.concept: complete enterprise-grade healthcare flow — patient create with PII, NHS number, AI risk scoring, audit, and governance proof
examples.aiRule: The enterprise summary combines every governance layer: secure flow, protected PII, AI inference, compute target, authority, policy, audit, and redact.

examples.concept: PII access requires pii.read effect
Protected value in response body requires policy
full contract model — all 16 sections in canonical order
examples.aiRule: The contract declares WHAT the flow is allowed, expected, and required to do. The body declares HOW.

examples.concept: full 16-section contract on a financial payment flow — all sections in canonical order
examples.aiRule: Financial flows must declare errors.redact for internal errors, privacy.contains PII, timeouts to prevent runaway execution, and require redaction before audit.write.

examples.concept: healthcare patient search with complete privacy, observability, errors, and context contract sections
examples.aiRule: Healthcare flows must declare privacy.contains PII, privacy.retention, deny protected values from logs, and require signed attestation for GDPR/NHS compliance.

examples.concept: medical domain — record types Patient and Medication, interaction validation pattern
examples.aiRule: Medical flows must use protected types for all PII (PatientId, MedicationCode), declare pii.read and audit.write effects, and redact protected values in audit entries.

examples.concept: scientific domain — Mass, Force, Energy domain record types, pure kinetic energy calculation
examples.aiRule: Scientific simulation flows are pure — no effects. Use Decimal for physical quantities to preserve precision.

examples.concept: readable equality alias
readable numeric comparison alias
readable boolean operators
unless as if-not alias
readable forms in governance conditions
